text: From 1990 to around 2010, almost all European countries underwent a shift to an all-volunteer military due to changing domestic pressures. However, in recent years the European security situation has deteriorated, leading to increasing discussions around reinstating conscription in a number of European countries. What is now often a 60-80-year-old conscription system has undergone considerable change to fit new societal realities and in an era of increased focus on individualism and neoliberal values. The conscription systems and their feasibility for present day are explored for six European countries: Estonia, Finland, Lithuania, Norway, Sweden and Switzerland. All of these have compulsory conscript military service, albeit with the exception of Lithuania and Sweden that reintroduced conscription just recently. The article investigates the various systems, and finds that whatever the actual system or structure of the conscription, all countries have a strong focus on individual voluntary conscription as well as on including women in the conscript forces. Hence, by looking at the relative size of the conscript classes; the motivations for conscript soldiers to actually serve; and the regulations and organizational arrangements made to ensure female conscripts are included, the article shows that conscription has adapted and can therefore also be seen as being a dynamic and thereby a viable conscription policy instrument. The conscription system and its present conditions are explored for six European countries.
